coredata.dc:description: The design of efficient synergistic therapeutic strategies for bacterial infection is highly desirable due to their high therapeutic efficiency and less side effects. In this study, a rapid and effective multi-mode antibacterial platform that integrates quaternary ammonium compounds (QACs), photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) was developed. QACs can induce initial damage to the bacterial cell membrane and render it more sensitive to heat. During 808 nm laser irradiation, the generation of hyperthermia and reactive oxygen species (ROS) can induce the death of damaged Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and the corresponding bacterial killing efficiencies are approximately 99.36% and 99.98%, which exceed single-mode therapy. In vivo antibacterial and anti-infective assays proved that effective sterilization could accelerate the infected wound healing process, and the traumas healed well within 14 days after treatment with Cu-RCDs-C35 and 808 nm irradiation. The wound healing area ratio was calculated to be 96%, which substantially exceeds the PBS + Laser, Cu-RCDs, and RCDs-C35+Laser groups. In addition, the cellular imaging experiment showed that Cu-RCDs-C35 could also serve as a fluorescence probe in bioimaging due to its inherent luminescence. Therefore, the present study provides high-efficiency and multifunctional carbon nanomaterials as antibacterial and bioimaging agents.

(C) XPS survey scan of Cu-RCDs-C35. (D) FTIR spectra of RCDs, CuCl2, Cu-RCDs, CAB-35, and Cu-RCDs-C35. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of RCDs-C35, CuCl2, and Cu-RCDs-C35. (F) PL spectra of Cu-RCDs-C35. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Thermal images of different doses of Cu-RCDs-C35 solution. (B) The temperature elevation corresponding to (A). (C) Temperature increases in Cu-RCDs-C35 solution (800 μg/mL) upon 808 nm irradiation at power densities of 1.5 W/cm2 (black line), 2.0 W/cm2 (red line), and 2.5 W/cm2 (blue line). (D) Recycling-heating profiles of Cu-RCDs-C35 solution (800 μg/mL) irradiated for six on/off cycles. (E) Time constant for heat transfer from Cu-RCDs-C35 was determined to be τs = 380.11472 s based on the linear time data from the cooling period (after 600 s) vs the negative natural logarithm of the driving force temperature. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A–C) UV–vis spectra of DPBF + RCDs-C35+Laser, Cu-RCDs-C35, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser at various times, respectively. (D) ESR spectra of TEMP + Cu-RCDs-C35 (red line), RCDs-C35+Laser (black line), and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser (blue line). (E–F) Fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA in E. coli and S. aureus cells with different treatments. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The relative cell viability of L929 cells under different treatments. Values are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). (B) Live/dead cells staining images of different treatment groups. (C) Confocal fluorescence images of Cu-RCDs-C35 in MCF-7 cells observed under 405 nm, 488 nm, 543 nm, and their merged images. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Optical images of RBCs from the positive control, negative control, 800 μg/mL of Cu-RCDs-C35 and RCDs-CuⅡ-C35. (B) The hemolysis rate of Cu-RCDs-C35 and RCDs-CuⅡ-C35 with different solution concentrations. Values are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photos of bacterial colonies of (A) E. coli and (C) S. aureus after being treated with Cu-RCDs, Cu-RCDs-C35, RCDs-C35+Laser, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser, respectively. The antibacterial ratio of different treatments to (B) E. coli and (D) S. aureus. Laser irradiation conditions: 2.0 W/cm2, 10 min. Data are presented as mean ± SD of three independent results. Marked with ∗∗ (p < 0.01) represented a significant difference. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus after being treated with Cu-RCDs, Cu-RCDs-C35, RCDs-C35+Laser, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser, respectively, without treated with samples as a control. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Live/dead bacterial cells staining images of (A) E. coli and (B) S. aureus after treatment with different conditions, where viable bacteria were stained weak blue by Hoechst 33342 and dead bacteria were stained red by PI (the scale bar is 20 μm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of efficient synergistic therapeutic strategies for bacterial infection is highly desirable due to their high therapeutic efficiency and less side effects. In this study, a rapid and effective multi-mode antibacterial platform that integrates quaternary ammonium compounds (QACs), photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) was developed. QACs can induce initial damage to the bacterial cell membrane and render it more sensitive to heat. During 808 nm laser irradiation, the generation of hyperthermia and reactive oxygen species (ROS) can induce the death of damaged Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and the corresponding bacterial killing efficiencies are approximately 99.36% and 99.98%, which exceed single-mode therapy. In vivo antibacterial and anti-infective assays proved that effective sterilization could accelerate the infected wound healing process, and the traumas healed well within 14 days after treatment with Cu-RCDs-C35 and 808 nm irradiation. The wound healing area ratio was calculated to be 96%, which substantially exceeds the PBS + Laser, Cu-RCDs, and RCDs-C35+Laser groups. In addition, the cellular imaging experiment showed that Cu-RCDs-C35 could also serve as a fluorescence probe in bioimaging due to its inherent luminescence. Therefore, the present study provides high-efficiency and multifunctional carbon nanomaterials as antibacterial and bioimaging agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection has become a severe issue that impacts public health [1–4]. The traditional therapeutic methods employ antibiotics to combat bacterial infection. However, the therapeutic effectiveness is significantly weakened following the excessive use of antibiotics due to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria and even superbacteria [5–7]. Therefore, the development of an efficient strategy for combatting bacterial infection is becoming of increasingly urgent necessity. In recent years, many nanomaterials have been exploited for antibacterial applications due to the rapid development of nanotechnology. For example, inorganic nanomaterials such as silver (Ag) [4,8], gold (Au) [9,10], titanium oxide (TiO2) [11,12], copper oxide (CuO) [13,14], and zinc oxide (ZnO) [15,16], along with natural organic antimicrobial peptides [17,18] and chitosan [19,20], have shown excellent antibacterial activities. Unfortunately, these nanomaterials can induce adverse effects by releasing metallic ions and exfoliated nanoparticles and can even damage the host cells [21]. Therefore, the design of an effective and safe antibacterial agent that does not produce resistance is essential.

In recent years, photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) have attracted substantial attention among various anti-infective therapies due to their minimal invasiveness, few adverse effects and localized treatment [22–24]. The two light-activated therapies employ radically different antibacterial mechanisms from traditional antibiotic therapy. For PTT, photothermal agents can convert light energy into heat with the assistance of near-infrared (NIR) light, which induce protein disruption and bacterial death. Additionally, PTT therapy has the potential to dramatically increase treatment efficiency since it can target the infected area. A temperature that exceeds 70 °C is required for realizing increased antibacterial efficacy [25]. However, single-mode PTT therapy with high temperature and long-term irradiation may lead to inflammation and thermal damage to nearby normal tissues. In PDT, NIR irradiation triggers the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radical (·OH), superoxide radical (·O2 −), and single oxygen (1O2), which can destroy microbial cells by disrupting cellular membranes, proteins, and nucleic acids, thereby realizing effective treatment [26]. Numerous ROS is needed to kill bacteria using a single PDT; however, excessive ROS could damage normal tissues by inducing inflammation and necrosis. In addition, the effectiveness of PDT is limited by the short lifetime of ROS, due to which the ROS do not significantly damage sufficiently many bacteria [27]. Therefore, it is necessary to coordinate PTT and PDT for antimicrobial treatment. Relatively low temperature and the small amount of ROS are better tolerated by tissue cells, inevitably compromising their antibacterial properties [28]. Synergistic therapy exhibits substantial potential for overcoming the disadvantages of the individual therapies to realize enhanced antibacterial performance.

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel nanomaterials in the field of nanomedicine with excellent biocompatibility, satisfactory water solubility, superstability, and unique luminescent performance in vitro and in vivo [29–31]. CDs have been widely applied to chemical and biological sensing, catalysis, drug delivery, biotherapy, and bioimaging due to their advantageous properties [32–34]. Recently, CDs, in combination with other materials, have been applied for bacterial detection, bacterial biofilm staining, and antibacterial fields based on their inherent luminescence and small size [35,36]. Additionally, CDs-based photoactivated strategies have attracted tremendous attention in the treatment of diseases, and most have been used in cancer therapy [22,29,34,37]. Few studies have reported CDs-based PTT/PDT synergistic therapy in the antimicrobial field. Most traditional CDs cannot satisfy the requirements of NIR light-mediated synergistic PTT/PDT therapy due to low absorption over the 650–900 nm area. Hence, the development of novel NIR-emitting carbon dots (RCDs) for PTT/PDT synergistic therapy is of urgent necessity. Although studies have reported that partial transition metal ions, such as CuⅡ, NiⅡ, and their complexes have NIR absorption properties [38–41], but their applications are severely limited due to their toxicities. It is considerable to decrease their toxicities for various biomedical applications. Hence, the combination of RCDs and transition metal ions through the N–Cu–N coordination mode to form Cu-doped RCDs (Cu-RCDs) is an excellent method for phototherapeutic applications. In addition, quaternary ammonium compounds (QACs) have an extensive history in the field of antibacterial due to their positive charges and hydrophobic chains [42–44].

Herein, we successfully prepared novel quaternized Cu-RCDs by conjugating Cu-RCDs with a quaternary ammonium compound, namely, cocoamidopropyl betaine (CAB-35), through a straightforward and friendly preparation route. CAB-35 contains a quaternary ammonium group and a long hydrocarbon chain, which can damage cell membranes [42,45,46], thereby rendering bacterial cells more sensitive to hyperthermia and ROS. The synthesized quaternized Cu-RCDs (Cu-RCDs-C35) combine PTT, PDT, and quaternary ammonium to realize synergistic antibacterial effects at low concentration, suitable temperature, and moderate ROS generation under 808 nm laser irradiation. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , we prepared novel NIR-emitting Cu-RCDs from citric acid, urea, and cupric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O) using the solvothermal method, in which dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was used as the solvent. Then, the CAB-35 molecules were covalently conjugated with Cu-RCDs via a carboxyl-amine reaction to produce Cu-RCDs-C35 with excellent antibacterial activity. Therefore, Cu-RCDs-C35 have substantial potential in the antibacterial field. Cu-RCDs-C35 could also be used for bioimaging due to its inherent luminescence. Interestingly, the as-prepared Cu-RCDs-C35 exhibited fascinating features compared to other reported modified CDs, which included the following: 1) the simple incorporation of copper into the core of RCDs to realize PTT/PDT synergistic therapy; 2) the combination with PTT, PDT, and quaternary ammonium could rapidly combat Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus); 3) synergistic therapy with low concentration, temperature, and ROS could promote wound repair and prevent inflammation; 4) a diversity of application fields, which include antibacterial and bioimaging. In summary, this study provides a novel strategy for advanced biomedical applications.

In a 0.1 M 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) buffer solution, 0.1 g CAB-35 was activated by N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC). The molar ratio of CAB-35: EDC: NHS was 1: 4: 4. Then, the mixture was added to 4 mL Cu-RCDs dispersions (5 mg/mL) after activation for 4 h. The reaction mixture was stirred for another 14 h and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to obtain Cu-RCDs-C35. After washing twice with ethanol and deionized water, respectively, the obtained black precipitate was resuspended in deionized water and dialyzed for 48 h with a cellulose dialysis bag (500–1000 Da) to remove residual solvents and organic molecules. Then, the purified product was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for further use. RCDs-CuⅡ-C35 were prepared via the same procedure.

The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of Cu-RCDs-C35 was determined by monitoring the temperature changes of Cu-RCDs-C35 solutions of various concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 μg/mL) under 808 nm irradiation (2.0 W/cm2, 10 min) at ambient temperature. Thermal photos and real-time temperatures were recorded using an infrared thermal imaging camera (FLIR ONE Pro, USA). The photothermal stability of Cu-RCDs-C35 was determined by irradiating for 10 min and naturally cooling, and the procedure was repeated six times. The photothermal conversion efficiency of Cu-RCDs-C35 was calculated in the light of previous literature [3]. Additionally, the photothermal property of RCDs-C35 was also evaluated, and the procedure was the same as the investigation of Cu-RCDs-C35.

The detection of 1O2 was performed using electron spin resonance (ESR) measurement and probe detection. ESR measurement: the glass capillary tubes containing the irradiated Cu-RCDs-C35 and RCDs-C35 (800 μg/mL, 50 μL) were inserted into the ESR cavity and the ESR signals was recorded, the 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as the spin trap was used to verify the generation of 1O2. Cu-RCDs-C35 without irradiating with 808 nm laser was used as a control group. Probe detection of 1O2: 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), which is a chemical 1O2 probe, was used to detect the generation of 1O2. RCDs-C35 and Cu-RCDs-C35 were dissolved in EtOH (800 μg/mL) and divided into RCDs-C35+Laser, Cu-RCDs-C35, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser groups. Then, the samples were blended with a DPBF solution (2 mM, 40 μL), respectively. The laser groups were irradiated by 808 nm NIR laser (2.0 W/cm2, 30 min), and the absorption intensity of DPBF at 410 nm was collected every 5 min. The Cu-RCDs-C35 group mixed with DPBF was regarded as a control group.

Intracellular ROS generation was detected using 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate dye (DCFH-DA). The bacterial suspensions of E. coli and S. aureus were mixed with a diluted DCFH-DA solution (λex = 488 nm, λem = 525 nm; 10 μM), respectively. After being cultured at 37 °C for 20 min with the bacterial suspensions inverted every 3 min, these mixtures were centrifuged (6000 rpm, 5 min) and resuspended into different doses of Cu-RCDs-C35 solution, respectively. After co-cultivation for 2 h at 37 °C, the fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA was observed at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm, which reflected the amount of ROS generation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial effect of Cu-RCDs-C35
ce:section.ce:para:
The antibacterial activity of Cu-RCDs-C35 was evaluated in terms of the number of colony-forming units (CFUs) using the plate-counting method. The two types of diluted bacterial solutions were incubated with the samples at various concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 μg/mL) for 24 h at 37 °C under 120 rpm rotation, respectively. Each bacterial mixture was divided into non-irradiation group and irradiation group, which included the Cu-RCDs group, Cu-RCDs-C35 group, RCDs-C35+Laser group, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser group. For irradiation group, after co-cultivation with RCDs-C35 and Cu-RCDs-C35 for 2 h, the mixtures were exposed to 808 nm laser irradiation (2.0 W/cm2, 10 min) and incubated in an incubator under the same conditions as the non-irradiation group. After treatment for 24 h, 100 μL of the bacterial suspension was taken and diluted 105 times. Then, the bacterial dispersions (30 μL) were spread on agar culture plates and cultured for another 24 h at 37 °C. This was performed in duplicate for each group, the colonies were counted to evaluate the antibacterial performance of the samples, and the experiment was conducted three times. The antibacterial ratio was calculated according to the following formula:
Antibacterial ratio=(CFUcg-CFUeg)/CFUcg × 100%
where CFUcg and CFUeg represent the numbers of colonies that formed in the control group and experimental group, respectively.

The morphologies of E. coli and S. aureus were observed via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) after treatment with PBS, Cu-RCDs (800 μg/mL), Cu-RCDs-C35 (800 μg/mL), RCDs-C35+Laser (800 μg/mL; 2.0 W/cm2, 10 min), and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser (800 μg/mL; 2.0 W/cm2, 10 min). 30 μL bacterial suspension was added to 3 mL volumes of the Luria-Bertani (LB) culture medium that each contained one of the samples, and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 16 h under 120 rpm rotation; the medium without the addition of sample group was considered a control. For irradiation group, after co-cultivation with RCDs-C35 and Cu-RCDs-C35 for 2 h, the mixtures were exposed to 808 nm laser irradiation (2.0 W/cm2, 10 min) and cultivated in an incubator under the same conditions as the non-irradiation group. After that, the bacteria were collected and washed twice with PBS, fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde at 4 °C under dark conditions for 4 h, and dehydrated with a series of ethanol/water mixtures (30, 50, 70, 90, and 100 vol%) for 15 min each time. Finally, the fixed bacteria were resuspended in deionized water, and 30 μL of the suspension was dropped on a silicon wafer and dried at room temperature. After treatment with platinum at 30 mA for 1 min, the morphologies of E. coli and S. aureus were observed using SEM.

Live/dead bacterial cells staining assay was also conducted to evaluate the antibacterial properties of Cu-RCDs-C35. First, E. coli and S. aureus were incubated with PBS, RCDs-C35 and Cu-RCDs-C35 for 6 h, which followed by 10 min irradiation, respectively, and non-irradiation group including Control group, Cu-RCDs group, and Cu-RCDs-C35 group were used as references. Then, the above bacterial suspensions were discarded after centrifugation (6000 rpm, 5 min), and the bacterial cells were washed with PBS for three times. Hoechst 33342 (100 μg/mL) was added and cultured for 10 min, then centrifuged and washed with PBS. Propidium iodide (PI, 100 μg/mL) was added and cultured for another 10 min at room temperature, and the mixture was centrifuged and resuspended in PBS. 10 μL of the suspension was dropped on a glass slide, covered with a cover glass, and visualized using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM).

S. aureus-infected mice model were built to evaluate the in vivo antibacterial and anti-infective effects of Cu-RCDs-C35, and all animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the institutional guidelines of Nanjing Normal University. The mice (Female Balb/c, 6 weeks) were anaesthetized via intraperitoneal injection with 10% chloral hydrate solution (0.3 mL/100 g), and the dorsal hair was wiped by hair removal cream. Then, the mice were disinfected with 75% ethyl alcohol, and wounds with a diameter of 0.8 cm were cut on the upper back sides of the spine. Subsequently, 100 μL of S. aureus (O.D.600 = 0.1) was inoculated over the wound region. These mice were divided into the following groups: 1) PBS + Laser; 2) Cu-RCDs; 3) RCDs-C35+Laser; 4) Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser. For the PBS + Laser group, 100 μL of PBS was inoculated into the wound and fastened with sterile nonwoven. For the Cu-RCDs, RCDs-C35+Laser, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser treatment groups, 100 μL of Cu-RCDs (800 μg/mL), RCDs-C35 (800 μg/mL), and Cu-RCDs-C35 (800 μg/mL) were applied and covered with sterile nonwoven, respectively. All irradiation groups were exposed to 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2, 5 min). The dressing was changed every 2 days for 14 days. The wounds were photographed at 0, 1, 3, 7, 10, and 14 days. The wound healing ratio was calculated via the following formula:
Wound healing area ratio (%) = 1-St / S0 × 100%
where St is the wound area of the mouse at 14 days and S0 is the wound area of the mouse at 0 days.

To analyze the extent of infection in the wound area, the plate-counting method was applied. The number of bacteria was determined by sampling at 1 day, 7 days, and 14 days using the wounds that corresponded to the infective mouse model. A sterile cotton swab was used to wipe the wound from one side to another side and immediately stirred in a sterile centrifuge tube that contained 2 mL of sterile PBS. 100 μL of the 103-fold diluted bacterial solution was spread on agar plates and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. The colonies were counted to evaluate the anti-infective performance of Cu-RCDs-C35.

The in vivo biosafety evaluation was conducted based on body weight and hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. The weight of each mouse needs to be recorded every day. After 14 days of treatment, all the mice were sacrificed, and their major organs and wound tissues were excised. The collected organs and tissues were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde solution, stained with H&E and photographed using optical microscopy.

Cu-RCDs-C35 were synthesized via a solvothermal method, and the process is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1. The obtained Cu-RCDs-C35 were uniform with the size of about 4.2 nm after calculation by counting more than two hundred particles. The morphologies were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images, which show that the obtained Cu-RCDs-C35 possess excellent dispersibility (Fig. 1 A). Furthermore, the HR-TEM image shows that the lattice spacing of the monodisperse Cu-RCDs-C35 is approximately 0.38 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystallographic facet of graphite. The hydrodynamic diameters of the Cu-RCDs-C35 were characterized via dynamic light scattering spectrometer (DLS), and the results demonstrated that the average diameter was 8.8 nm. In fact, the result was consistent with TEM owing to DLS measured overall hydrodynamic size (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1B, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of RCDs, Cu-RCDs, and Cu-RCDs-C35 show one broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.98°, 27.67°, and 23.09°, respectively. The results demonstrated that the three samples consist of graphene-like structures, which correspond to the (002) crystal plane diffraction peak. Additionally, compared with the XRD diffraction patterns of RCDs, a lower diffraction peak can be observed in Cu-RCDs and Cu-RCDs-C35, and accompanied by further broadening, indicated that amorphous character of the particles was increased. In addition, the increment of the interplanar distance may be attributed to copper doping, as was found in nitrogen-doped CDs [47]. The interlayer spacing of Cu-RCDs-C35 was calculated to be 0.38 nm via the formula of 2dsin θ = nλ, which was consistent with the HR-TEM image. The zeta potential of as-synthesized Cu-RCDs and Cu-RCDs-C35 was also measured by DLS. The zeta potential of Cu-RCDs was approximately −33.7 mV, and that of Cu-RCDs-C35 was approximately −10.7 mV (Fig. S2). The changes of zeta potential indicated that CAB-35 successfully modified the surface of Cu-RCDs and produced Cu-RCDs-C35. The elemental component and chemical bond composition of Cu-RCDs-C35 were detected via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS). In the full XPS spectra (Fig. 1C), peaks at 168.05 eV, 197.23 eV, 284.8 eV, 399.32 eV, 530.93 eV, and 931.93 eV confirm the presence of S2p3/2, Cl2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Cu2p [48–51]. According to the XPS data, the chemical compositions of the Cu-RCDs-C35 are C 68.21%, N 13.2%, O 13.85%, S 1.61%, Cu 1.12%, and Cl 2.01%, indicated that Cu has been successfully doped into RCDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. S3A) can be deconvolved into four peaks, which correspond to the signals of CO (287.7 eV), C–S (286.1 eV), C–N (285 eV) and C–C/CC (284.5 eV). The N1s spectrum (Fig. S3B) shows three prominent peaks at 401.85 eV, 399.5 eV, and 399 eV, which are associated with pyrrolic N, amino N, and pyridinic N, respectively. The O1s bands contain two peaks at 531.6 and 530.86 eV, which originate from O–CO/N–CO and CO, respectively (Fig. S3C). High-resolution S2p spectra (Fig. S3D) indicates the presence of O–SO (168.8 eV), N–SO (167.9 eV), and CS (163.3 eV). The Cu2p peaks (Fig. S3E) that are centered at 932 and 952 eV can be attributed to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2, respectively. The Cl2p spectra (Fig. S3F) displays two peaks at 197.25 and 198.88 eV, which are associated with Cl2p3/2 and Cl2p1/2, respectively. The chemical bonds of RCDs, CuCl2, CAB-35, Cu-RCDs, and Cu-RCDs-C35 were characterized via FTIR. As shown in Fig. 1D, the absorption peak at 3443 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibrations of –OH and N–H bonds, and the absorption peaks at 2927 and 2854 cm−1 are due to C–H symmetric stretching and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the long methylene chain of the CAB-35 moiety. The absorption bands at 1640 and 1549 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CN and CC, respectively. The peaks at 1397 and 1190 cm−1 were attributed to C–N stretching vibrations and C–OH scissoring vibrations, respectively. Compared with RCDs and Cu-RCDs, the bands of Cu-RCDs-C35 at 1021 cm−1 could be assigned to the N–Cu–N stretching vibration, which is consistent with the peak at 1017 cm−1 of Cu-RCDs. The amounts of conjugated CAB-35 on the Cu-RCDs were determined by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As displayed in Fig. S4, the total weight losses of Cu-RCDs and Cu-RCDs-C35 after heating to 800 °C were approximately 65% and 77%, respectively, and the grafting rate of CAB-35 was calculated to be approximately 12%.

The optical properties of Cu-RCDs-C35 were verified by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Strong optical absorption in the NIR region for an ideal phototherapeutic agent is crucial, which could convert light to heat energy or transfer energy to the surrounding oxygen, thereby leading to the generation of ROS. As shown in Fig. 1E, Cu-RCDs-C35 exhibit a broad peak at 770–900 nm and a maximum absorption peak at 815 nm, which is consistent with the absorption of CuCl2. However, the absorption peak was not observed for RCDs-C35. This analysis demonstrated that Cu-RCDs-C35 were successfully synthesized. Additionally, the broader absorption of Cu-RCDs-C35 ranging from UV–vis to NIR region is attributed to RCDs-C35, suggesting the Cu-RCDs-C35 and RCDs-C35 with superior photothermal effects. The excitation-independent behavior of Cu-RCDs-C35 was investigated and described in Fig. 1F. An obvious dual-emission phenomenon was found under excitation at various wavelengths from 330 to 450 nm, and the emission obviously redshifted from 452 to 656 nm to 530 and 900 nm, respectively. The peaks after 656 nm could enhance the intensity of NIR-emission for Cu-RCDs-C35. The possible reasons for the dual-emission phenomenon are 1) the influence of partial nanoparticle agglomerates [48]; 2) the effect of quantum size, which is attributed to organic solvents affecting the degree of dehydration and carbonization process [52]; 3) the mutual competition between different emission centers and traps; 4) the formation of more molecular states and surface states due to the functional groups on the surface of Cu-RCDs-C35. The maximum PL intensities of Cu-RCDs-C35 were appeared at 480 and 704 nm when excited at wavelengths of 390 and 350 nm. This result demonstrated that the emission of Cu-RCDs-C35 is excitation-wavelength-independent. Using quinine sulfate as a reference (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4), the photoluminescence quantum yield of Cu-RCDs-C35 was calculated to be 0.65%. The result indicated that Cu-RCDs-C35 could act as an ideal candidate for photothermal nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and photodynamic properties of Cu-RCDs-C35
ce:section.ce:para: Due to NIR absorption, Cu-RCDs-C35 could be utilized as a promising photothermal agent. The photothermal properties of Cu-RCDs-C35 were explored under 808 nm irradiation (2.0 W/cm2, 10 min), and deionized water was served as a control. As depicted in Fig. 2 A–B, the temperature of Cu-RCDs-C35 (800 μg/mL) increased quickly up to 57.3 °C; in contrast, no obvious temperature changes were observed in pure water. Meanwhile, the power-dependent temperature curves were also monitored and plotted in Fig. 2C. The temperature was increased from 28 °C to 45.4 °C, 57.3 °C, and 61.5 °C with power densities of 1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 W/cm2, respectively. These obtained results indicated that Cu-RCDs-C35 could efficiently convert the absorbed laser energy into heat. The photothermal stability of Cu-RCDs-C35 was evaluated by measuring the temperature of the solution for six cycles of the recycling-heating process. As shown in Fig. 2D, the temperature showed no significant changes after six cycles of laser on/off, thereby indicating the super heat reproducibility and photothermal stability of the Cu-RCDs-C35 solution. To calculate the photothermal conversion efficiency of Cu-RCDs-C35, the temperature variation of Cu-RCDs-C35 as a function of time was recorded. As displayed in Fig. 2E, η was calculated to be 32.47% through the fitting parameters of the cooling stage, which is a comparable efficiency to those of previously reported CDs (Table S1). In addition, the photothermal property of RCDs-C35 was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S5, the RCDs-C35 exhibited good photothermal performance under irradiation of 808 nm laser and the photothermal conversion efficiency was calculated to be 29.51%, which was lower than Cu-RCDs-C35, suggested that the doping of Cu enhanced the photothermal effect of RCDs-C35. These obtained results suggested that Cu-RCDs-C35 has the potential for application in the biomedical field as an efficient photothermal agent.

To evaluate the PDT activity of Cu-RCDs-C35, DPBF was served as a chemical trapping agent to determine the generated ROS. ROS such as 1O2 can react with DPBF to generate oxidation products, leading to decreased absorption at 410 nm [53]. As shown in Fig. 3 A–B, the absorbance intensity of DPBF shows no apparent changes after treatment with RCDs-C35+Laser and Cu-RCDs-C35, while the absorbance intensity of DPBF gradually decreases after treatment with Cu-RCDs-C35 under 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 3C). These obtained results demonstrated that following 808 nm laser irradiation, Cu-RCDs-C35 could effectively produce 1O2 and that the structure of N–Cu–N plays a crucial role in the generation of 1O2. To prove the above results, ESR was also performed to detect free radicals that were generated by Cu-RCDs-C35. TEMP was used to trap 1O2. As displayed in Fig. 3D, no TEMP/1O2 signals were detected without Cu doping (black line); after doping Cu into RCDs, the TEMP/1O2 signals were obviously detected in the presence of 808 nm laser irradiation (blue line), while no significant TEMP/1O2 signals were detected in the absence of 808 nm laser irradiation (red line). These results indicated that 1O2 was generated by energy transfer from the excited triplet state of Cu-RCDs-C35 to ground-state oxygen (3O2) after excitation by 808 nm laser irradiation; moreover, Cu doping in Cu-RCDs-C35 improved the process of energy transfer from Cu-RCDs-C35 to O2 [34].

The intracellular generation of 1O2 by Cu-RCDs-C35 under 808 nm irradiation was also evaluated in bacterial cells using DCFH-DA, which is a cell-permeable non-fluorescent probe. In the presence of 1O2, DCFH-DA can be oxidized to dichlorofluorescein (DCF), which possesses high fluorescence intensity. The corresponding fluorescence intensity after co-cultivation with E. coli and S. aureus was detected and displayed in Fig. 3E–F. Compared with non-irradiation groups, the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced following 808 nm laser irradiation. However, nearly no fluorescence changes were measured in deionized water with or without 808 nm irradiation. These results indicated that following the irradiation of 808 nm, Cu-RCDs-C35 could prominently generate ROS and possess the potential for application in the biomedical field as a photodynamic agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay and cellular imaging of Cu-RCDs-C35
ce:section.ce:para: Before applied in antibacterial and bioimaging fields, the biocompatibility of Cu-RCDs-C35 in L929 cells was first evaluated via MTT and Calcein-AM/PI co-staining assays. As revealed in Fig. 4 A, negligible cytotoxicity was identified after the treatment of Cu-RCDs-C35, and the cell viabilities exceeded 85% even as the concentration reached 800 μg/mL. Additionally, the phototoxicity was also evaluated. Under 808 nm laser irradiation, the cell viabilities were approximately 84% and 73% after treatment of Cu-RCDs-C35 and RCDs-CuⅡ-C35, respectively, with concentration of 800 μg/mL, thereby suggesting that the biocompatibility of Cu-RCDs-C35 is better than RCDs-CuⅡ-C35. The obtained results may be attributed to the coordination of CuⅡ and RCDs. In Cu-RCDs-C35, CuII was retained in the inner of RCDs via N–Cu–N complexation, while CuII may located in the outer of RCDs through OC–O–Cu–O–CO complexation in RCDs-CuII-C35. The generated hyperthermia could promote the release of CuII after 808 nm laser irradiation for RCDs-CuII-C35, leading to the slight cytotoxicity.

The obtained results from live/dead cells staining were the same as those of the MTT assay. As displayed in Fig. 4B, bright green fluorescence signals were detected in every group, and weak red fluorescence signals were also detected after treatment with Cu-RCDs-C35, while no red fluorescence signals were readily observed for the control group. Compared with the Cu-RCDs-C35 group, the red fluorescence signals were not enhanced after treatment with Cu-RCDs-C35 and 808 nm laser irradiation, suggested that Cu-RCDs-C35 possess excellent biocompatibility and negligible phototoxicity. In addition, the cell imaging capability of Cu-RCDs-C35 was explored via human breast cancer cells (MCF-7). As presented in Fig. 4C, the cells exhibited blue, green, and red fluorescence when excited with 405, 488, and 543 nm laser pulses using CLSM, which suggested that Cu-RCDs-C35 possess excellent photoluminescence performance and could be used in the bioimaging field. These results demonstrated that Cu-RCDs-C35 could be considered as a potential candidate in the biological field.

The hemocompatibility of Cu-RCDs-C35 was evaluated via hemolysis assay, and the morphologies of treated blood cells were imaged via microscope. Fig. 5 A displayed the corresponding optical images of RBCs that were treated with deionized water, normal saline solution, Cu-RCDs-C35, and RCDs-CuⅡ-C35. Compared with the negative control, the RBCs that were treated with Cu-RCDs-C35 and RCDs-CuⅡ-C35 exhibited negligible changes in morphology, which were marked with yellow and green dashed circles, respectively; however, the positive control exhibited rupture of RBCs. As depicted in Fig. 5B, the hemolysis ratios of Cu-RCDs-C35 and RCDs-CuⅡ-C35 were less than 5%; however, the hemolysis ratios of Cu-RCDs-C35 were lower than RCDs-CuⅡ-C35, suggested that the hemocompatibility of Cu-RCDs-C35 is better than RCDs-CuⅡ-C35. These results demonstrated that Cu-RCDs-C35 possess super hemocompatibility to RBCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity assay of Cu-RCDs-C35
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro synergetic antibacterial activity of Cu-RCDs-C35 against Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus was evaluated via the spread plate technique, MTT assay, bacterial morphologies, and live/dead bacterial cells staining. As displayed in Fig. 6 A and C, many viable colonies emerged on LB agar plates in all groups when the concentration of the samples was 0 μg/mL. For non-irradiation group, enhanced antibacterial effects were observed as the concentrations of Cu-RCDs and Cu-RCDs-C35 were increased; the antibacterial ratio for E. coli reached 35.2% and 62%, respectively; and the antibacterial ratio for S. aureus reached 35% and 66%, respectively, when the concentration was 800 μg/mL (Fig. 6B and D). The obtained results demonstrated that quaternary ammonium groups and hydrophobic chains on the surface of Cu-RCDs-C35 could damage the bacterial membrane, leading to the death of bacteria. Compared with the Cu-RCDs-C35 group, the RCDs-C35+Laser group displayed a higher antibacterial ratio due to the synergistic PTT and quaternary ammonium, which reached 80% and 82% for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The Cu-RCDs-C35 +Laser group displayed the best antibacterial effect, and the antibacterial ratio reached 99.36% and 99.98% for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, as calculated in Fig. 6B and D, which could be attributed to triple synergistic sterilization including quaternary ammonium, PTT, and PDT. These results demonstrated that the antibacterial efficiency of multi-mode with quaternary ammonium, PTT, and PDT exceeds that of single-mode, suggested that Cu-RCDs-C35 possess the potential in multi-mode antibacterial application fields.

The membrane integrity of E. coli and S. aureus before and after treatment with Cu-RCDs-C35 and NIR irradiation was also investigated by SEM. As shown in Fig. 7 , the cell walls and membranes of E. coli and S. aureus in the control group were smooth and intact; after the addition of Cu-RCDs, E. coli and S. aureus were slightly rough (yellow arrows) due to the protonation of amino groups on the surface of the Cu-RCDs. After co-cultivation with Cu-RCDs-C35, the two bacteria were rough and wrinkled (red arrows) due to quaternary ammonium damaged the cell membranes. In addition, the cell membranes of both bacteria were significantly damaged (yellow dashed circles) after treatment with RCDs-C35 and 808 nm laser irradiation, suggested that quaternary ammonium and hyperthermia exerted a synergistic antibacterial effect. It is noticed that E. coli and S. aureus suffered the most severe membrane damage (red dashed circles) in the presence of Cu-RCDs-C35 and NIR irradiation, indicated that quaternary ammonium, hyperthermia, and ROS could destroy and oxidize the membranes of the two bacteria, thereby leading to the death of the bacteria. These results proved that quaternary ammonium could cause initial damage to bacterial membranes through electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, which made the bacteria more sensitive to hyperthermia and ROS, leading to the leakage of intracellular matrix, such as proteins and nucleic acids, and causing the destruction of the bacteria. These results confirmed that the NIR-irradiated Cu-RCDs-C35 were highly efficient in killing bacteria.

Besides, live/dead bacterial cells staining test was also conducted to evaluate the antibacterial performance. The viable bacteria with intact membranes were stained blue fluorescence by Hoechst 33342. The dead bacteria with damaged membranes were stained red fluorescence by PI. As displayed in Fig. 8 , both E. coli and S. aureus in the Control and Control + Laser groups were stained blue, and weak red fluorescence was observed, indicated that the treatment with only the laser negligibly affected the viability of the bacteria. After co-cultivation with Cu-RCDs, the red fluorescence signals of E. coli and S. aureus were slightly enhanced, indicated that the two bacterial membranes suffered minor damage due to the protonation of amino groups on the surface of the Cu-RCDs. Moreover, in the Cu-RCDs-C35 and RCDs-C35+Laser groups, the red fluorescent signals of the two bacteria were apparently enhanced, suggested that quaternary ammonium and PTT played essential roles in sterilization. It is noticed that the red fluorescence signals of E. coli and S. aureus were significantly increased, and almost no blue fluorescence signals were observed after treatment with Cu-RCDs-C35 and 808 nm laser irradiation, suggested that Cu-RCDs-C35 possess excellent antibacterial capability under 808 nm laser irradiation. The results of the in vitro antibacterial activity assay proved that Cu-RCDs-C35 could synergistically eradicate bacteria through quaternary ammonium, PTT, and PDT.

Inspired by the excellent in vitro bactericidal efficacy and biocompatibility, the in vivo photo-assisted therapeutic effectiveness of Cu-RCDs-C35 on open wounds was also evaluated, and the healing progress of infected wounds was recorded. Fig. 9 A displayed photos of abscesses on days 1, 3, 7, 10 and 14 in the different treatment groups. All groups displayed a certain degree of infection in wounds after inoculation with S. aureus on day 1. On day 3, compared with the PBS + Laser group, the Cu-RCDs, RCDs-C35+Laser, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser groups exhibited lower degrees of infection. After 7 days of treatment, the abscess around the wounds disappeared, and the infected areas of the wounds had scabs; moreover, the scars of irradiation groups gradually darkened. On day 7 post-treatment, the scars of the Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser group slowly fell off, and the other groups exhibited no such phenomena, suggested that PTT and PDT played indispensable roles in sterilization and wound healing. After treatment for 14 days, the scars vanished, and the Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser group exhibited an excellent healing effect compared with the other groups, in which the wound was almost closed. Furthermore, the ichor was still visible inside each wound in PBS + Laser, Cu-RCDs, and RCDs-C35+Laser groups. The wound healing area ratios of the PBS + Laser, Cu-RCDs, RCDs-C35+Laser, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser groups were calculated to be 62%, 70%, 85%, and 96%, respectively. Furthermore, the inflammatory response was investigated by H&E staining, which could reflect the process of wound healing. As shown in Fig. 9A, re-epithelialization, neo-blood vessel formation, and mature hair follicles formation were readily observed in the Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser group after treatment for 14 days. These features could reflect the degree of wound healing [54]. In contrast, the epidermis was thin, the collagen fibers were disordered and loose, and even newborn hair follicles were immature in the PBS + Laser, Cu-RCDs, and RCDs-C35+Laser groups, which reflected slower wound-healing processes. In addition, the thicker and more mature tissues revealed more collagen deposition in wounds after treatment with Cu-RCDs-C35 and 808 nm laser irradiation, suggested that Cu-RCDs-C35 could enhance fibroblast migration to accelerate wound healing [55–57].

To quantitatively evaluate the antibacterial and anti-infective capability of Cu-RCDs-C35, the bacteria around the wounds on days 1, 7, and 14 were wiped and cultured on agar plates. The therapeutic efficacy was evaluated by comparing the number of colonies in the infectious region. As shown in Fig. 9B, the RCDs-C35+Laser group had fewer colonies than the PBS + Laser and Cu-RCDs groups, suggesting the synergistic action of PTT and quaternary ammonium. Compared with the other groups, the Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser group showed the lowest number of bacterial colonies, indicated that the combination of PTT, PDT, and quaternary ammonium was more effective for sterilization and could promote the process of wound healing. Additionally, the temperature increases on wounds that were treated with Cu-RCDs-C35 under 808 nm irradiation was recorded by an infrared thermal imaging camera. As shown in Fig. 9C, the abscess site temperature of the rat that was treated with Cu-RCDs-C35 increased from 37.6 to 56.8 °C, and the control group had no obvious changes after 5 min of 808 nm laser irradiation, suggested that Cu-RCDs-C35 could serve as a photothermal therapeutic agent.

The in vivo biosafety was evaluated via body weight measurement and H&E staining. In Fig. S6, the body weight change trends of the mice in the Cu-RCDs, RCDs-C35+Laser, and Cu-RCDs-C35+Laser groups were consistent with the PBS + Laser group, suggesting negligible in vivo toxicity. In addition, H&E staining images showed that the major organs, which included the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys, had no significant damage compared with the PBS + Laser group, indicated that Cu-RCDs-C35 exhibited excellent biocompatibility in vivo (Fig. 10 ). Both in vitro and in vivo results demonstrated that Cu-RCDs-C35 possess super antibacterial capability and excellent therapeutic effect; thus, they have high potential for application in the biomedical field.

In conclusion, a novel phototherapeutic agent with PTT, PDT, and quaternary ammonium for effectively killing bacteria and accelerating the healing process of infected wounds was developed via solvothermal method, and this phototherapeutic agent was named Cu-RCDs-C35. Our results demonstrate that Cu-RCDs-C35 possesses broad-spectrum antibacterial properties against E. coli and S. aureus due to the collaboration of NIR-mediated hyperthermia, ROS generation, and electrostatic attraction. Moreover, Cu-RCDs-C35 could also act as fluorescence cell imaging agent for cancer cells visualization due to their inherent luminescence. The results of both in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrate the excellent biosafety of Cu-RCDs-C35. The different behavior of Cu-RCDs-C35 under NIR light irradiation with normal cells and bacteria might be attributed to that the cilia and flagella spread all over the surface of the bacteria [58], making large number of Cu-RCDs-C35 attached to the surface, thereby leading to the death of bacteria under irradiation through triple synergistic sterilization effect, but the normal cells would not attract Cu-RCDs-C35. Moreover, the in vivo anti-infective capabilities were successfully proven in an infective mouse model, and Cu-RCDs-C35 could effectively sterilize and accelerate the healing process of infective wounds under 808 nm laser irradiation. Additionally, single NIR-laser-triggered nanomaterials that exhibit PTT/PDT synergistic antibacterial properties could overcome the complexity of multiple light sources. These findings demonstrate that Cu-RCDs-C35, as a NIR-laser-triggered phototherapeutic nanomaterial, contribute to the development of therapeutics for bacterial infection-based carbon nanomaterials that are suitable for wound healing treatments and show considerable potential in biomedical applications.
